zones.name: Rex PRD Management CLI
zones.description: A self-contained CLI tool and MCP server for managing hierarchical product requirements documents, including code analysis, tree operations, validation, persistence, and AI-agent workflow integration.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 71% of project files (36/51) — may be too broad, consider splitting
The dependency graph flows cleanly from schema -> core -> store -> cli with no circular dependencies, indicating strong architectural discipline for a 36-file single-zone codebase.
The analyze module (scanners, propose, reconcile) is a well-isolated subsystem that could be extracted into its own zone if the project grows, since it has minimal coupling to core.
The MCP server in cli/mcp.ts at ~405 lines is the largest single file and serves as a second entry point alongside the CLI dispatcher, making it a natural seam for future zone splitting.

The store layer (file-adapter.ts) imports core/tree.js and core/canonical.js, creating a dependency inversion: store depends on core business logic instead of being a dumb persistence layer. Layering should be schema ← core ← store, but store reaches sideways into core.
PRIORITY_ORDER is duplicated three times (core/canonical.ts, core/next-task.ts, analyze/propose.ts) — a hidden coupling via copy-paste that risks drift if priority tiers change.
CLI commands init.ts and validate.ts bypass the store abstraction by directly using node:fs/promises for config file I/O, undermining the adapter pattern.
The core layer has no barrel export (no src/core/index.ts), so consumers import from individual files (core/tree.js, core/next-task.js), making the public API surface implicit rather than explicit.
REX_DIR constant is duplicated: declared in cli/commands/constants.ts and re-declared in cli/mcp.ts, creating a sync risk for the two entry points.

sortByPriority logic is duplicated between core/canonical.ts:14-27 (sortItems, recursive) and core/next-task.ts:11-18 (sortByPriority, shallow) — two subtly different sort implementations within the same core layer that can diverge.

MCP tool handlers in cli/mcp.ts reimplement the same orchestration logic as CLI commands (e.g., get_next_task duplicates cmdNext, update_task_status duplicates cmdUpdate) rather than sharing a common service layer, creating a parallel maintenance burden across ~400 lines.

The PRDItem interface in schema/v1.ts:21 has an index signature `[key: string]: unknown` alongside explicit fields, which defeats TypeScript's excess-property checking and allows arbitrary data to pass type checks silently — the `.passthrough()` on the Zod schema compounds this by accepting unknown keys at runtime too.

readLog() at store/file-adapter.ts:105 casts each parsed JSON line as LogEntry without validation (`JSON.parse(line) as LogEntry`), while appendLog() validates via Zod before writing — asymmetric serialization boundary means corrupted log files can inject malformed entries into the system.

The analyze --accept flow in cli/commands/analyze.ts:125-169 calls store.addItem() sequentially in a loop (one file write per item) with no batching or transaction, so a failure mid-loop leaves the PRD in a partially-updated state with no rollback.

update.ts:6-12 hardcodes VALID_STATUSES and VALID_PRIORITIES as string Sets instead of deriving them from the Zod enums in schema/validate.ts (ItemStatusSchema, PrioritySchema), creating a second source of truth that will drift if enum values change.
callSourceVisionMCP in recommend.ts:33-78 uses raw child_process.spawn with callback-based stream handling, but never sets a timeout — a hung sourcevision process will block the CLI indefinitely.

The PRDItemSchema in validate.ts:18 is typed as `z.ZodType<Record<string, unknown>>` erasing the inferred Zod type, so validateDocument returns `Record<string, unknown>` forcing every callsite to cast `as PRDDocument` — the Zod schema is well-typed internally but the export discards the type information.

PRIORITY_ORDER is defined three times (canonical.ts:7, next-task.ts:4, propose.ts:25) with two different type signatures — propose.ts uses Record<string, number> (loose) while the other two use Record<Priority, number> (strict), creating a silent type-safety gap
The adapter field in RexConfig is dead configuration — all 8 createStore() call sites hardcode the literal string "file" instead of reading config.adapter, making alternative adapters unreachable

Log event names for the same semantic operation differ between CLI and MCP: update.ts:68 emits "item_updated" while mcp.ts:129 emits "status_changed" for status changes, with no shared event-name constants
init.ts and validate.ts both bypass the store abstraction layer, using raw fs readFile/writeFile directly, making them incompatible with any non-file adapter
[call graph] 698 internal calls, 148 outgoing, 487 incoming (cohesion: 0.83, coupling: 0.17)

zones.name: Analysis Engine
zones.description: Core analysis pipeline including file inventory, import graph extraction, Louvain community detection, route parsing, AI enrichment, and CLI orchestration.
insights: High cohesion (0.94) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (0.94) reflects a well-structured pipeline where analyzers, CLI commands, schema constants, and utilities form a tight internal dependency chain.
zones.ts orchestrates Louvain clustering, AI enrichment, and insight generation—consider splitting algorithmic and AI-enrichment concerns for testability.
The CLI commands/ directory acts as the orchestration layer calling all analyzer phases sequentially, making it the natural entry point for the entire tool.
src/util/ (sort.ts, merge.ts) is exclusively consumed by analyzers—this utility layer has zero viewer or test imports, reinforcing its role as an analyzer-private subsystem.
All analyzers accept typed parameters and return values rather than reading/writing JSON directly; the CLI commands layer handles all file I/O, keeping analyzers pure and composable.

enrich.ts (925 lines) has 7-8 distinct responsibilities: prompt construction, AI process lifecycle, JSON parsing, finding extraction, batch orchestration, meta-evaluation, and result merging — it should be split into at least 4 focused modules.
enrich.ts accepts `inventory` and `imports` parameters that are never read inside the function body — these are dead parameters that mislead callers about the function's data dependencies.
enrich.ts contains dead code at line 815-816: a ternary expression `(condition ? undefined : undefined)` that always evaluates to `undefined` regardless of branch.
next-steps.ts is a new untracked analyzer with zero test coverage — no unit, integration, or e2e tests exist for it anywhere in the test suite.
Rate-limit errors from the AI CLI are detected but trigger immediate retry with no backoff delay, burning through all 3 attempts instantly before giving up.
SV_DIR and TOOL_VERSION are independently defined in 3 files (cli/commands/constants.ts, analyzers/manifest.ts, cli/mcp.ts) — version drift risk if any is updated without the others

detectCirculars is implemented twice with near-identical DFS logic: src/analyzers/imports.ts:232 and src/util/merge.ts:129 — the imports.ts comment references merge.ts but re-implements rather than importing
computeSummary (inventory.ts:641) and computeInventorySummary (merge.ts:36) are functionally identical with different names — classic DRY violation

GENERIC_PREFIXES (inventory.ts:345) and GENERIC_SEGMENTS (zones.ts:29) serve the same purpose but differ: zones includes 'pkg', inventory does not — a file under pkg/ gets inconsistent category vs zone ID

enrich.ts (925 lines) is a god module mixing 7 responsibilities: pass config, prompt building, timeout computation, Claude CLI invocation, JSON parsing, finding extraction, batch orchestration + retry — highest maintenance burden in the codebase
_updatedFindings bypasses the EnrichResult interface via 'as' casts in enrich.ts:703 and zones.ts:496 — type safety escape hatch instead of extending the interface properly
metaEvaluationCount exists in schema/v1.ts:154 but is absent from the Zod validation schema in schema/validate.ts — writing succeeds but strict validation would strip or reject it
commands/analyze.ts (313 lines) imports from 8+ modules and repeats a 4-phase pattern of readFileSync→JSON.parse→analyze→writeFileSync — orchestration is monolithic with high cascade risk
Barrel export src/analyzers/index.ts omits enrich, louvain, context, llms-txt, and next-steps — false expectation of completeness, and no consumer actually imports via the barrel
[call graph] 1074 internal calls, 86 outgoing, 67 incoming (cohesion: 0.93, coupling: 0.07)

zones.name: Interactive Viewer
zones.description: Preact single-page application that visualizes analysis results through force-directed graphs, zone maps, route trees, and findings panels, consuming only schema types with zero analyzer dependencies.
insights: High cohesion (0.96) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 43% of project files (46/107) — may be too broad, consider splitting
Contains 43% of project files (46/106) — may be too broad, consider splitting
Highest cohesion (0.96) and lowest coupling (0.04) confirm the viewer is fully decoupled from the analysis engine, communicating only through serialized JSON conforming to schema/v1.ts.
The component/view separation follows a clean hierarchy: views import components, components are pure props-driven, and loader.ts is the single data ingestion point.
schema-compat.ts provides forward/backward compatibility for data format changes, which is essential since the viewer may load data produced by different analyzer versions.

The viewer imports schema/validate.ts directly (not through the barrel index.ts), creating an implicit dependency on Zod at bundle time—validate.ts and its Zod dependency are compiled into the viewer bundle even though schema/index.ts re-exports the same symbols.

The viewer's separate esbuild entry point (build.js) with Preact JSX transform means it cannot share compiled artifacts with the Node-side CLI/analyzer code—changes to shared schema types require rebuilding both targets.

views/graph.ts (628 lines) contains a 436-line imperative renderGraph function that manually constructs SVG DOM, binds event listeners, and manages mutable closure state — the physics extraction into graph/ was started but rendering was never extracted alongside it.

The LOD rendering logic (circle radius clamping, font-size formula, label visibility) is duplicated verbatim between the tick callback (lines 365-390) and the wheel handler (lines 420-441) in graph.ts.
The `meterClass` function is defined identically in both views/zones.ts and components/detail-panel.ts with the same 0.7/0.4 threshold constants — a simple copy-paste that should be a shared utility.
The `basename` extraction pattern (`.split('/').pop() || x`) appears 11 times across 6 viewer files — a missing utility function.
sidebar.ts inlines two full base64-encoded PNG logos as JavaScript string constants, bloating the component and preventing image optimization or lazy-loading.
View export naming is inconsistent: Overview and Graph lack the 'View' suffix used by all 6 other views (ZonesView, FilesView, ArchitectureView, ProblemsView, SuggestionsView, RoutesView)
ZONE_COLORS[i % ZONE_COLORS.length] modulo-index pattern is repeated 9 times across 5 files — no shared getZoneColor() utility exists

graph.ts at 627 lines is a split-brain architecture: Preact component (lines 38-189) manages state while renderGraph() (lines 192-627) does imperative SVG DOM creation with deeply interleaved closure-scoped mutable state

Record<string, unknown> is used as the detail panel data contract across 8 call sites in 5 files — relies on duck-typing (typeof detail.path === 'string' && !detail.zoneId) instead of a discriminated union
fileToZone map construction is independently implemented in graph.ts, files.ts, and detail-panel.ts — same iteration over zones.zones with slightly different shapes
FlowDiagram data preparation (pairCounts from crossings) is copy-pasted identically between views/zones.ts:59-78 and views/architecture.ts:54-74
.split('/').pop() || path appears 11 times across 7 files as ad-hoc basename extraction — no shared utility

CSS has unused classes: .zone-insights (cards.css:88-113), .component-tree/.component-node (cards.css:258-287), .route-node (routes.css:10-20), .route-layout (routes.css:52-56) — never referenced from TypeScript

zones.name: Test Fixtures
zones.description: Self-contained sample TypeScript project used as input data for integration and end-to-end tests, intentionally isolated with no imports from or exports to production code.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling (0.0) confirm this is a fully self-contained fixture project that exists solely as test input data.
No entry points means no production code imports these files—they are consumed only by test runners scanning the fixtures/ directory path.
The small-ts-project fixture models a realistic micro-application (models, services, utils, config) providing representative input for analyzer pipeline tests.
small-ts-project fixture (7 files) lacks edge cases: no circular dependencies, no binary files, no deeply nested directories, no non-TypeScript sources
remix-app fixture (5 files) only covers file-based routes — no config-based routes, no splat routes, no optional segments, no error boundaries
Fixtures are only used by integration/e2e tests; all unit tests create their own inline temp directories and files rather than referencing the fixture directories
[call graph] 5 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Test Suite
zones.description: Unit, integration, and end-to-end tests covering analyzer logic, schema validation, CLI workflows, and the serve command, with imports reaching into both analyzer and viewer zones.
insights: High coupling (0.54) — 4 imports target "analysis-engine"
Low cohesion (0.46) is expected for a test zone—tests naturally fan out across the codebase to import the modules they verify rather than forming tight internal clusters.
High coupling (0.54) correctly reflects that tests are consumers of production code; the 4 imports into analyzers and 1 into viewer match the test coverage distribution.

schema/validate.ts landing in this zone suggests it is primarily exercised by tests rather than production code paths—consider importing it in the CLI validate command to strengthen its production-zone gravity.

validate.ts is now imported in both the CLI validate command and the viewer loader, contradicting its previous test-zone gravity—it belongs logically in the schema layer shared by all three production zones.
The `renderToDiv` helper function is copy-pasted identically across all 5 viewer test files instead of being extracted to a shared test utility module.

Test data factory functions (makeFileEntry, makeInventory, makeEdge, makeImports) exist only in zones.test.ts while integration tests for llms-txt and context manually construct the same data structures verbosely.

The integration tests for llms-txt.ts and context.ts use synthetic inline data objects rather than running the actual pipeline — they are effectively unit tests misplaced in the integration directory.
zones.test.ts (1265 lines) is a mega-test spanning 3 source modules (zones.ts, louvain.ts, enrich.ts) — no dedicated louvain.test.ts or enrich.test.ts exist
components.test.ts (696 lines) tests both components.ts and route-detection.ts — no route-detection.test.ts exists, making route detection tests hard to discover
Core graph algorithms louvainPhase1, mergeBidirectionalCoupling, and capZoneCount have zero direct unit tests — only exercised indirectly through integration-level analyzeZones tests
Pure functions tryParseJSON, buildMetaPrompt, and getPassConfig in enrich.ts are trivially testable but completely untested
validateImports and validateZones are imported in validate.test.ts but have zero test cases — malformed data could silently pass validation
[call graph] 72 internal calls, 10 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0.88, coupling: 0.12)

Inconsistent error handling patterns across orchestration files - some use process.exit(1) while others throw errors
Mixed error handling strategies: cli.js uses process.exit(1), config.js throws Error objects, and web.js uses .catch() patterns - standardize to consistent error handling approach

Viewer-side PRDItemData type is an intentional duplicate of rex types for browser compatibility, but creates maintenance burden if types diverge
CSS files contain unused classes (.zone-insights, .component-tree, .route-node, .route-layout) that are never referenced, indicating dead code

All zones show perfect internal cohesion (1.0) indicating excellent module organization within each architectural tier
Zero cross-zone imports detected suggests either gateway pattern usage or analysis limitation in capturing runtime dependencies
Perfect cohesion across all zones with zero detected coupling indicates exceptionally clean architectural boundaries
Four-tier architecture implements strict layered dependency flow: orchestration → execution → domain → foundation
Gateway pattern successfully isolates cross-package imports to single modules (hench/prd/ops.ts, web/mcp-deps.ts)
Domain packages (rex, sourcevision) maintain complete independence with zero cross-imports detected

Domain layer isolation pattern allows rex and sourcevision to evolve independently while sharing foundation infrastructure
Four-tier architecture enforces clean unidirectional dependency flow with no circular dependencies detected
Interface layer violates DRY principle by duplicating tree traversal functionality instead of using gateway pattern consistently
Schema validation modules (validate.ts) are misplaced in test zones when they serve production functionality across multiple packages
God modules exist at critical architectural points (enrich.ts 925 lines, MCP handlers 400 lines) creating maintenance bottlenecks and violation of single responsibility
Sub-zone fragmentation in execution layer contradicts perfect cohesion claims - internal architectural inconsistencies suggest zone boundaries need refinement
Multiple packages duplicate utility functions (basename extraction, tree traversal, data preparation) instead of using shared abstractions or gateway pattern consistently
God modules at architectural boundaries (enrich.ts 925 lines, MCP server 400 lines) create maintenance bottlenecks and violate single responsibility principle
Codebase exhibits 'documentation inflation' with 768 JSDoc blocks across 230 files - suggests over-documentation that may hinder code readability
Error handling consistency gap: orchestration layer mixes process.exit(), Error throws, and .catch() patterns without documented conventions
Architectural paradigm inconsistency: interface layer heavily uses classes while execution/domain layers prefer functional patterns
No TODO/FIXME/HACK comments detected across entire codebase - either exceptional code quality or potential suppression of technical debt documentation
Console.log statements concentrated in orchestration files (86 occurrences) suggest debugging artifacts that should be replaced with structured logging
Wildcard imports (`import * as`) are rare (3 occurrences) indicating good import discipline, but build scripts use them unnecessarily
Dead code concentration pattern: unused exports cluster in adapter/integration modules (notion-adapter.ts, validate.ts, logos.ts) suggesting incomplete feature integration or over-abstraction
Maintenance burden triangle: God modules (enrich.ts 925 lines), God functions (analyzeZones 88 calls), and hub functions (walkTree 20 callers) create three distinct maintenance bottlenecks
Gateway pattern effectiveness: Only 2 cross-package gateways manage 322 files successfully, but utility duplication suggests the pattern should extend to shared foundation abstractions

Inverse correlation between architectural quality and feature completeness: perfect zone boundaries with zero coupling coexist with significant dead code, suggesting strong design discipline but incomplete feature integration

Dead code accumulation in integration layers: 39+19+9+8+7+5+4=91 unused exports concentrated in adapter, validation, and UI component modules indicates systematic over-engineering at system boundaries

Implement shared utility extraction: move basename extraction, tree traversal, and data preparation functions from packages/web, packages/rex, and packages/sourcevision into @n-dx/claude-client foundation to eliminate triplication
Architectural quality paradox: perfect zone isolation coexists with internal hotspots, suggesting strong package boundaries but weak internal modularization
Dead code follows the 80/20 rule: web package contains 85% of unused exports (78/91), indicating systematic over-engineering in UI/adapter layers vs core domain logic
Critical path convergence: tree.ts + chunked-review.ts + analyzeZones function create a dependency triangle affecting 138+ files - refactoring priority should target this cluster

Systematic over-engineering at system boundaries: dead code concentrates in adapters (notion-adapter.ts 19 exports), UI components (renderer.ts 39 exports), and validation layers (validate.ts 8 exports) while core domain logic remains clean

Hotspot cascade effect: tree.ts fan-in (30 files) + walkTree hub function (20 files) + chunked-review coupling (370 calls) create a critical dependency bottleneck affecting 50+ files across rex and dependent packages

Implement graduated refactoring strategy: 1) Extract tree.ts operations into focused modules, 2) Break chunked-review test coupling, 3) Decompose analyzeZones god function, 4) Remove dead code from web package adapters
